content.full_text:
California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
August 6′  ■990
Gl,en H. spain, Esq.
Glen H. Spain, Inc.
Professional, Lar,, corporation
P. O. Box 205
Conptche, CA 95427
Dear Mr. spain:
You have requested advice on behalf of three clients regard-
ing the pernissible uses of campaign funds under the Po1itical
Reiorm ntt (tne "Act).1 Your ciieit= are the cornmittee for
Proposition :o2 lthe "Proposition 3O connittee'r), the Forest and
wiliilife Protection committee3 (the "Protection committeert)', and
Forests Forever, a nonprofit corporation (the trcorporation'r ) .
OUESTION
After the campaign for which they are organized j-s concluded,
can the Proposition 30 Committee and the Protection conmittee
donate their renraining carnpaign funds to Forests Forever, a tax-
exenpt, nonprofit California corporation?
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our Fil.e No. I-90-410
1 co.r".rrr"nt Code Sections 810oo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Goverrulent code un].ess otherwise indicated. 
Corumission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of ReguLations Section
l-8ooo, et seq. A11 references to reguJ.ations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2 Yo,l have informed me that this conmittee is now in the process
of changing its name to the committee for Proposition 130.
3 Th" current name of this committee is rrForests Forever Comnit-
tee, A Coalition of Conservation organizations. rr However you have
stated that this conmittee is in the process of anending j.ts name
to the one above.
4 Th" two committees shall be collectiveLy referred to as the
ttcommittees. tl
428 J Street,Suite 800 ● P.0.Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804´ 0807 ● (916)322‐ 5660


our File No. r-9 o-410
Page 2
CONCLUSION
A donation from the Protection Coromittee to the corporation
is perrnitted under the Act. A donation froro the Proposition 30
conmittee may be pernitted under the Act if the donation will not
have a material financial effect upon the committee's treasurer.
FACTS
The committees are orgranized to support the Forest and
wildlife Protection and Bond Act of 1990, a statewide baIlot
measure which wiLl be on the baIlot on November 6' l99o (the rrbal-
1ot measurerr). After the election it is anticipated that the con-
mittees will terminate. After the canpaign, and after the paynent
of all campaign exPenses there will probably be carnpaign funds
renaining.- the coronittees plan to donate the reroaining campaign
funds to the corPoration.
The corporation is a tax-exempt nonprofit corporation. under
California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 237O]-f and United
States InternaL Revenue code Section 50L(c) (4). 
Among other
thi.ngs, the purposes of the corporation as stated in its Articles
of Iicorporation incLude the production and distribution of
educational materials concerning conservation and use of natural
resources and the qualifying and effecting of California baflot
initiatives. 
The Corporation also expects to take an active role
in enforcing the ballot measure, if it is approved, or
alternatively to iurplement the provisions of the ballot measure
through adninistrative means or by other ballot measures.
The current treasurer for the corporation is also currently
the treasurer for the Proposj.tion 30 Conmittee. He is also a
member of the board of diiectors of the corporation. As discussed
in our telephone conversation of June 29 t ]-9go, the treasurer is
currently a vofunteer. Your letter also states that any later
compensaLion wouLd be set by vote of the board of directors of the
corporation (excluding hinself) and rrould be a fixed amount
regirdless of whether or not excess canpaign funds are donated.
ANALYSI S
Sections 85800-85806 set forth the pernitted uses of campaj-gn
funds held by a bal.lot measure comnittee. Section 85803 provides:
carnpaign funds may be used to nake donations
or loans to bona fide charitable, educational,
civic, religious, or similar tax-exenpt, nonprofit
organizations, where no substantial part of the
proceeds will have a material financial effect on
the candidate, elected officer, nember of his or
her inrnediate fanily, or the canpaign treasurer,
and where the donation or loan bears a reasonable
relation to a political, 
legislative, or
governmental purpose.
Thus the Act provides that a ba1lot measure committee may use
its canpaign funds to rnake donations to nonprofit organizations
provided three requirements are net. First, the recipient


our File No. I -9 0-410
Page 3
organj.zation must be a bona fide charitable, educational , civic,
religious, or simifar tax-exempt nonprofit orgTanization. Second,
no substantial part of the proceeds of the donation can have a
material financia] effect on the candidate or elected officer,
members of his or her imrnediate faruily, or the treasurer of the
committee. Third, the donation must bear a reasonable relation-
ship to a political, 
legislative, or governmental purpose. (Sec-
tion 85803; see Lee Advice Letter, No. I-90-214, copy enclosed.)
with regard to the first requirement, you have represented
that the corporation is tax-exenpt under both state and federal
1aw (Revenue and Taxation Code 237olfi Internal Revenue code
501(c) (4), respectively). 
We believe that the corporation
therefore meets this requirement.
with regard to the second requirement, you have stated that
the treasurer for the Proposition 30 committee wil,1 also be a
treasurer for the corporation. The second requirement prohibits
any substantial part of the proceeds of the donation having a
material financial effect on the treasurer of the bal1ot neasure
cornmittee. (section 85803.)5 while the commission has not
defined the term rrmateriaf financial effect'r in the context of
uses of campaign funds, it has done so in the context of the Act/s
regutation of conflicts of interest. 
Analogy to Cornmission
regulations on this subject may therefore provide guidance as to
whether a ball-ot measure cornmittee's donation of campaign funds
has a materiat financial effect on that coEmittee's treasurer.
Generally, Commission regulations find that a transaction has a
rnaterial financial effect on an official 
if it increases his or
her j,ncome or assets by at least S25o or more. (See Regul'ation
La702.1(a) (4) copy enclosed.) Therefore, if a ballot measure
committee's donation of campaign funds increases the income or
assets of the commj-ttee's treasurer by $250 or more, the donation
will Iikely be deemed to have a naterial financial effect on the
treasurer ind be prohibited.5 
(see Lee Advice Letter, suDra;
Stathan Advice Letter, I-89-690, copy enclosed.)
' 
Section 85803 provides that no substantial part of the
proceeds donated to he tax-exempt organization may have a material-
financial effect on the candidate or elected officer $rho controls
the connittee, a member of his or her irnnediate faurily, or the
carnpaign treasurer. The intent of this provision would appear to
be to prohibj,t persons $rho control the expenditure of funds by a
committee from benefiting from those expenditures. consistent
with this interpretation, when applying Section 85803 to
noncandidate controlled comrnittees, we believe that the provision
should be viewed as a guideline prohibiting expenditures which
materially benefit any person who has significant influence over
expenditure decisions of the comnittee. we have not been
presented with any facts regarding other persons involved lrith the
committees and accordingly express no advice as to thern.
5 
Th" commission nay be considering regulations on this point
in the near future which may alter this advice.


our File No. I-90-410
Page 4
Your letter states that the treasurer's compensation wouLd be
a fixed amount regardless of srhether or not excess campaign funds
are received. rf the treasurer's compensation is not fixed and
rather is dependent on the amount of donation from the conmittees,
then the donition may be prohibited.T As you and r discussed in
our telephone conversation on June 29, 1990, the compensation the
treasurer will- receive is not fixed at present' The corporation,
however, is amenable to fixing the conpensation prior to any dona-
tion by the Proposition 30 Committee. We belj,eve that such
protection is necessary to prevent the treasurer's corupensation
frorn being in any way a function of the Proposition 30 committee's
donati.on. such amount should be set well before the anount of the
donation is determined. As we currently do not have the terms of
the treasurer's compensation before us, we cannot advise you on
whether the donation fron the Proposition 30 committee meets this
requ j.rement . 8
In respect to the donation by the Protecti.on Committee, under
the facts presented to us the Protection Committee's donation does
not appear to have any financial effect on the treasurer of the
Protecti.on Comnittee.
The third requirement, $rhether the donations bear a reason-
able relationship to a political, 
legislative or governnental
purpose, depends on the particular facts of the donor's situation.
lseL statfran Advice Letter, supra.) Here the donations bear a
reasonable relationship to a political purpose. First, one of the
purposes of the corporation is to qualify and effect ballot
initiatives 
and to conduct political 
campaigns in support of such
initiatives. 
Second, if the ballot ueasure passes, then the
corporation rrexpects to take an active rolerr in enforcing the
provisions of the ba11ot measure. In our telePhone conversation
of Juty 24, L99o, you explained this stateuent to nean that the
corporation intends to defend any legal challenges to the enacted
baI1ot measure and take lega1 or administrative actions necessary
to ensure that the provisions of the ba1lot measure are properly
inplenented by the charged regulatory agencies. Third, if the
ballot measure fai1s, the corporation trexpectsrr to inpJ.ement the
provisions of the ba1Iot measure through adrninistrative means or
by other ba1lot neasures. So Iong as the monies are used for
.,' 
while your letter states that the treasurer will not receive
anything more than what is fair and reasonable for actual services
rendered, this is not the standard under section 85803.
8 
without this information, we cannot provj.de you lrith fornal
written advice and thus only provide informal assistance in this
letter. 
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with
the innunity provided by an opinion or fornal written advice.
(covernment Code Section 831114i 1 cal. code of Reqs. Section
18329(c) (3).) 
You may wish to provide clarification 
later in
writing.


Our Fi■e No.  I-90-4■0
Page 5
these three purposes the donation bears a reasonable relationship
to a politicll, 
purpose.9
civen the above stated conditions, a donation from the con-
mittees to the corporation will be permissible under the Act.
However, we call to your attention Elections code sectior] 29'795
which nay also restrict the use of caropaign funds collected by
your clients. 
The Elections code is interpreted and enforced by
the Attorney General's office, and I suggest you contact them
regarding your clients' obligations under the Elections code.
I trust that this letter has provided you with the guidance
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this nat-
ter, please contact ne at (916) 322-5901.
s incerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel
,--/-,4 J*l-*"
By: Mark Morodomi /
counsel, Legal Division
SH : llM: dg
Enclosures
o- This interpretation of the political purpose requirenent is
linited to the requirement,s apptication to ballot measures.
Other considerations obtain when the political purpose requirement
is applied to candidates or elected officials.
